summary: I was born in 1955 and spent the first year of my life in Havelock while my parents built our house on Harkers Island. Havelock is about thirty-five miles west of the island and is home to Cherry Point, the Naval air rework facility. My daddy worked at Cherry Point, which was a major economic driver for the area after World War II, providing jobs that helped many families like mine.

My family has owned land on Harkers Island for generations, and I grew up in the house my parents built there. It’s a small, close-knit community where fishing was once king, but over time, economic changes have shifted the landscape. My mother worked for the Rural Electrification Administration here, which later became the Electric Membership Co-op. Growing up on Harkers Island shaped much of my early life and values.

The Rural Electrification Administration, or REA, was the power company on Harkers Island when my mother started working there after I finished high school. It later became the Electric Membership Co-op. My mother balanced her job there with being a dedicated church volunteer and school mother. The REA was important in bringing electricity and modernization to our rural community.

The Electric Membership Corporation is what the Rural Electrification Administration became over time on Harkers Island. My mother worked there after high school, helping to provide electricity to our community. This organization was vital in supporting the infrastructure and daily life on the island, especially as it modernized from its fishing roots.

entity: Marine Corps Air Station Cherry Point
summary: Cherry Point, located in Havelock about thirty-five miles from Harkers Island, was where my daddy worked. It’s a Naval air rework facility built after World War II that became the economic salvation of the area. Many men from the island worked there before returning to fishing or shrimping, and it provided a more stable income for families like mine, even though my dad’s job was mostly unskilled labor.

Camp Lejeune is another military facility mentioned alongside Cherry Point that provided jobs for men in our community after World War II. Like Cherry Point, it was part of the shift in the local economy from fishing to military-related work. Many men would work at these bases and then return home to fish or shrimp, balancing both livelihoods.

I went to school on Markers Island starting in 1961, where the population was around twelve to fifteen hundred people. It was a small, tight-knit community with one first grade and one second grade class, so we all grew up together. The island had a strong fishing economy with scallop houses, fish houses, restaurants, and motels, though over time the population has declined and land prices have changed the community dynamics significantly.

I attended the Methodist Church on Markers Island, which was one of many churches in the area. Church life was a big part of the community and often divided people socially, but it also connected many families through shared faith and activities. My mother was very involved as a church volunteer, which influenced my upbringing and values.

Markers Island, Atlantic, Smyrna, and Beaufort are all part of Carteret County, where I grew up. The county school system shaped much of my education, especially when I was selected to attend high school early as part of a gifted program. Over time, changes in population and land values in Carteret County have forced school district reorganizations and altered community life significantly.

Atlantic is a neighboring community to Markers Island in Carteret County. It had a strong commitment to education, with many students being college-bound, unlike Markers Island which was more focused on fishing and boat building. Atlantic also had two students in the gifted eighth-grade program I attended, and later I learned that Atlantic Christian College, where I went to school for a year, is located there.

Smyrna is another small community in Carteret County where middle school students from Markers Island and Atlantic now attend after district reshuffling. When I was growing up, Smyrna had five students in the gifted eighth-grade program, more than Markers Island or Atlantic. Smyrna is part of the broader down east community that shaped much of my schooling experience.

Beaufort is the wealthier, more politically powerful town visible from Markers Island, but culturally very different from the down east communities like mine. Most of the gifted eighth-grade students came from Beaufort, and it was intimidating to be from Markers Island when I joined that group. Beaufort has always been somewhat separate socially and economically from the fishing communities down east.

Many of the kids in my gifted eighth-grade class were children of teachers or staff from Duke University’s lab school, which contributed to the academic environment I experienced. Although I never attended Duke myself, the influence of its community was felt in the educational opportunities and competition I encountered during that pivotal year. It was a big change from Markers Island and helped set my course toward teaching gifted education later on.

I wanted to attend Appalachian State University because I was interested in going to the mountains, but my mother did not allow me to go because it was too far away. This was part of the family influence on my educational path, which ultimately led me to attend Atlantic Christian College instead. Although I never got to experience Appalachian State, it was a school I seriously considered during my college search.

I ended up attending Atlantic Christian College in Atlantic, North Carolina, but I only stayed for one year because I hated it. It was not what I expected, and I struggled to find my place there. This experience was part of my early adult life before I eventually pursued teaching gifted education, reflecting the challenges of leaving my small community for college.
